# **Autonomous AI-Driven DNS Filtering: Architectures for Proactive Adblocking, Behavioral Verification, and Evasion Resilience**

The modern digital ecosystem is characterized by an escalating conflict between user privacy initiatives and increasingly sophisticated advertising and malicious delivery infrastructures. As traditional, list-based Domain Name System (DNS) filtering reaches the limits of its efficacy, the necessity for an autonomous, AI-driven approach becomes apparent. A system capable of monitoring commonly connected domain requests, verifying blocks through behavioral analysis before deployment, and maintaining functionality through periodic integrity checks represents the current frontier in network-layer defense.1 This report examines the technical requirements and architectural paradigms of such a system, focusing on the integration of deep learning models, kernel-level monitoring, and adaptive evasion strategies.

## **The Paradigm Shift from Static to Autonomous DNS Filtering**

The foundational mechanism of DNS filtering has historically relied on Domain Name System Blocklists (DNSBL) or Real-Time Blackhole Lists (RBL). These services operate as curated databases of IP addresses and domains known for engaging in spam, hosting malware, or serving intrusive advertisements.5 Traditional implementations involve a mail server or network gateway performing a DNS query against a blocklist provider. If the queried domain returns an A record, typically in the 127.0.0.0/8 loopback range, the connection is rejected or flagged.5 While these systems are highly automated and capable of handling millions of detections daily, they are fundamentally reactive. A domain must first be identified and reported before it can be added to the list, creating a window of vulnerability.6

Advertisers and malicious actors exploit this latency through techniques such as Domain Generation Algorithms (DGA) and CNAME cloaking.8 DGAs utilize pseudo-random algorithms to generate thousands of potential command-and-control (C2) domains, of which only a few are registered at any given time, making it impossible for static lists to remain comprehensive.8 Furthermore, CNAME cloaking leverages the DNS system to hide third-party tracking domains behind first-party subdomains, bypassing blockers that only monitor known third-party entities.10

The emergence of AI-driven autonomous adblocking addresses these limitations by shifting the focus from "who" is being blocked to "how" the domain behaves and is constructed. By monitoring real-time DNS traffic and applying machine learning (ML) models, the system can identify and mitigate novel threats the moment they appear, often before they are even used in a campaign.1

## **Architectural Components of AI-Driven Domain Classification**

The core of an autonomous DNS blocker is its ability to perform high-precision classification of domains without prior knowledge. This process is divided into feature extraction, model inference, and real-time policy enforcement.1

### **Lexical and Structural Feature Engineering**

The initial phase of classification involves the extraction of lexical and structural features from the domain string. This provides a "fingerprint" of the domain that reveals markers of automation or malicious intent.4 A critical metric in this analysis is Shannon entropy, which quantifies the randomness of the character distribution within a domain name.4

The formula for Shannon entropy ![][image1] is expressed as:

![][image2]  
In this equation, ![][image3] represents the probability of the occurrence of a specific character ![][image4] within the string ![][image5]. Malicious domains generated by automated tools often exhibit significantly higher entropy than human-curated domains.4 However, sophisticated attackers may attempt to bypass this by using dictionary-based DGAs, which create pronounceable but randomized strings. To counter this, models analyze word segmentation ratios and linguistic patterns to distinguish between legitimate business names and machine-generated sequences.1

| Feature Category | Specific Metrics | Security Implication |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Lexical Randomness | Shannon Entropy, Gini Impurity | High values correlate with automated DGA and encrypted tunneling payloads. |
| Structural Build | Length, Subdomain Depth, TLD Suffix Ratio | Excessive subdomains or unusual length-to-suffix ratios flag tracking hierarchies. |
| Character Patterns | Digit Count, Hyphen Density, Sequence Length | Suspicious symbol usage often indicates phishing or typosquatting attempts. |
| Linguistic Validity | Vowel-to-Consonant Ratio, N-gram Frequency | Identifies machine-generated strings that lack human-readable linguistic structures. |
| Proximity Scoring | Levenshtein Distance, Jaro-Winkler | Measures similarity to high-reputation domains to detect brand impersonation. |

Detailed structural analysis also considers the depth of the subdomain hierarchy. While legitimate Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) use subdomains for load balancing, trackers frequently use them to segment data collection.9 The ratio of the domain name length to its top-level domain (TLD) suffix is another red flag; domains that are excessively long relative to their suffix are statistically more likely to be used for data exfiltration or as C2 channels.4

### **Deep Learning Architectures for DNS Pattern Recognition**

To move beyond static rules, autonomous systems employ advanced neural network architectures that can learn complex, non-linear relationships within DNS traffic. These models are typically trained on massive datasets containing both benign traffic from ISPs, schools, and businesses, and malicious traffic from known attack campaigns.1

A high-performance approach utilized in industry environments involves the deployment of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) autoencoder.1 The autoencoder is trained exclusively on millions of legitimate domains to learn the latent representations of normal DNS behavior. During real-time operation, when a new domain query is received, the autoencoder attempts to reconstruct the input. The difference between the input and the reconstructed output, known as reconstruction loss, serves as a primary feature. If the reconstruction loss exceeds a predefined threshold, the domain is flagged as an anomaly.1

For sequential data, such as a series of DNS queries from a single host, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks are preferred. LSTMs are designed to handle temporal dependencies by selectively retaining or forgetting information across time steps.14 This is essential for detecting "low and slow" data exfiltration attacks where data is broken into query-sized chunks and sent at low frequencies to avoid detection by traditional rate-limiting monitors.13

The Attention-CNN-LSTM hybrid model represents the state-of-the-art in this domain.15 In this architecture:

1. **CNN Component:** Responsible for the extraction of spatial features, such as the local character patterns within a domain name.16  
2. **LSTM Component:** Models the temporal sequence of the DNS queries, identifying patterns of communication that suggest a command-and-control relationship.14  
3. **Attention Mechanism:** Highlights the most informative input features, ensuring the model focuses on the most critical markers of malicious intent while ignoring noise in the traffic.3

The implementation of such a model in a production environment, specifically a four-layer deep learning model designed by Splunk, demonstrates the efficacy of this approach. This model utilizes an input layer of 98 features, followed by two dense linear layers with 256 units each using Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation functions.13 A dropout of 0.5 is applied between layers to prevent overfitting. The final output layer uses a Sigmoid activation function to convert the processed signals into a probability score, indicating the likelihood that an instance is a malicious exfiltration attempt.13

## **Verifying Blocklist Updates and Mitigating Website Breakage**

A major deterrent to the adoption of aggressive DNS filtering is the risk of "overblocking," where legitimate network resources are inadvertently obstructed, leading to broken website functionality or application failures.2 An autonomous AI adblocker must implement a verification pipeline to assess the impact of a block before it is committed to the production environment.17

### **Automated Regression Testing for Web Compatibility**

The primary challenge in automated verification is that "brokenness" is subjective and can manifest in subtle ways, such as a non-functional form on a deeply nested page or a blank container where a critical asset should load.17 To address this, autonomous systems utilize a classification pipeline trained on the commit history of major filter projects like EasyList.17

By analyzing "fix" commits—where a filter maintainer adds an exception rule to unblock a previously blocked resource—the system learns to identify the characteristics of rules that cause breakage. The automated system then uses a heavily modified Chromium-based browser to execute the target web page twice: once with the new block applied and once without it.17 The browser records script execution, DOM node modifications, and event listener registrations, exporting this data as an XML-encoded directed graph.17

| Compatibility Metric | Predictive Value for Breakage | Detection Mechanism |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| DOM Node Deltas | High | Tracks the number of elements inserted or removed by scripts after blocking. |
| Event Handler Failure | Very High | Monitors whether interaction listeners (e.g., click, submit) are missing. |
| Resource Chain Gaps | Moderate | Identifies if a blocked script was the "actor" responsible for loading other assets. |
| Visual Diffing | Emerging | Compares screenshots for blank spaces or broken layouts (proposed extension). |
| Text-to-Image Ratio | Low | Detects content-heavy pages that fail to render correctly without style sheets. |

This process extracts over 430 features from the page execution recordings. A classifier implemented in scikit-learn, trained on this dataset, can predict website breakage with an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.88.17 This allows the autonomous blocker to flag "high-risk" rules for manual review or to automatically adjust the rule's scope (e.g., converting a global block into a domain-specific block) to preserve functionality.17

### **Periodic Health Monitoring and Reputation Decay**

Domains in the digital ecosystem are dynamic; a domain used for malicious purposes today may be repurposed for legitimate use in the future, or its malicious content may be removed.7 An autonomous system must perform periodic checks to maintain the accuracy of its blocklist and avoid stale entries.7

The Spamhaus Domain Blocklist (DBL) provides a model for this behavior. It is highly automated, and most listings expire automatically once the associated malicious activity is no longer detected.7 If a domain is used in legitimate traffic for a sufficient period to establish a good reputation, the system notices the change and removes the listing.7 Autonomous blockers implement this through "reputation scoring," where domains are tracked over time. A domain's score is influenced not just by its current behavior but by its history and its association with known malicious IP reputations.22

Monitoring tools like Oh Dear and UptimeRobot can be integrated into the autonomous workflow to provide real-time status updates.21 These tools check domains against 11 major blocklists and monitor for unexpected changes in DNS records (A, AAAA, MX, TXT). If a domain resolved correctly yesterday but now returns an NXDOMAIN or SERVFAIL error, the system can trigger an investigation to determine if the authoritative server is down or if the domain has been hijacked.23

## **Advanced Evasion and Detection Bypass Strategies**

As ad-blocking and security filtering become more prevalent, the entities being blocked have developed "anti-adblock" techniques to detect and neutralize filtering attempts.11 A sophisticated autonomous DNS blocker must operate with stealth and counter these evasion tactics at the network layer.

### **Bypassing Anti-Adblock via DNS Manipulation**

Websites typically detect adblockers using JavaScript-based triggers. These include "bait elements"—small DIVs with class names like adsbox that are likely to be hidden by cosmetic filters—and "script execution checks" that verify if an ad-related JavaScript file (e.g., ads-prebid.js) successfully loaded.26 If the script is blocked, the website detects the adblocker and may present an "adblock wall," blurring content or restricting access.25

An AI-driven DNS blocker can bypass these checks through several mechanisms:

1. **Null Response Optimization:** Instead of dropping a packet or returning an NXDOMAIN error—which is easily detectable by a script—the resolver can return a null IP (0.0.0.0) or point to a local, trusted server that serves an empty, innocuous file.25  
2. **Resource Replacement:** The system can identify requests for common tracking scripts and replace them with "stubs" or "shims." These are modified versions of the scripts that provide the expected API surface to the website's code but do not perform any tracking or ad-serving.19  
3. **CNAME Uncloaking:** Since trackers use CNAME records to appear as first-party resources, the resolver must recursively follow the alias chain to reveal the true destination.11 By revealing that analytics.example.com is actually a CNAME for tracker.com, the system can block the request at the DNS level, which is invisible to the browser's DOM-based detection scripts.9

| Detection Method | Website Tactic | DNS-Level Countermeasure |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Bait Elements | Checking if .adsbox is hidden. | Cosmetic filtering is browser-based; DNS allows the shell to load but blocks the payload. |
| Script Verification | Testing if ads.js executed. | Return a null response or a local stub to satisfy the load event without tracking. |
| Network Monitoring | Catching failed requests. | Use an AI model to identify and allow the detection script while blocking the ad server. |
| CNAME Cloaking | Hiding trackers in subdomains. | Recursive uncloaking to reveal and block the canonical third-party domain. |
| Server-Side Insertion | Embedding ads in content. | Behavioral analysis to detect unusual packet size or timing indicative of ad-streams. |

The "cat-and-mouse" game extends to fingerprinting. Websites use browser fingerprinting—collecting data on screen resolution, installed fonts, and device specifications—to identify users even when cookies are cleared.11 An autonomous DNS system can mitigate this by blocking access to the telemetry and fingerprinting endpoints that these scripts use to exfiltrate the gathered data.30

### **Countering Server-Side Ad Insertion (SSAI)**

Server-Side Ad Insertion (SSAI), also known as "ad stitching," is one of the most difficult evasion techniques to counter because it embeds advertisements directly into the content stream at the server level.2 Because the ad and the content are served from the same domain and often the same IP, traditional DNS filtering is ineffective.

However, an AI-driven system can monitor "commonly connected domain requests" to identify patterns of traffic that suggest SSAI.4 By analyzing the volume, frequency, and packet size of requests to content delivery domains, machine learning models can detect anomalies. For instance, a video stream that periodically requests small, encrypted chunks from a secondary subdomain may be flagged as fetching ad-segment keys.13 While DNS blocking cannot stop the ad once it is stitched, it can block the initial handshake with the ad-decision server that facilitates the stitching process.32

## **Real-Time Performance and High-Efficiency Implementation**

The utility of an AI-driven DNS blocker is strictly bounded by its performance. DNS resolution is a critical path for all network activity; any significant delay introduced by AI inference will result in a degraded user experience, often referred to as the "silent killer" of network services.33

### **Kernel-Level Filtering with eBPF and XDP**

To achieve the necessary performance, modern DNS filtering is moving from userspace proxies to kernel-level enforcement using Extended Berkeley Packet Filter (eBPF) technology.34 eBPF allows developers to run sandboxed programs inside the Linux kernel without modifying the source code or loading unstable modules.34

By attaching eBPF programs to the eXpress Data Path (XDP), DNS packets can be processed the moment they arrive at the network interface, before they are even parsed by the kernel's network stack.34 This eliminates the overhead of context switching between the kernel and userspace, allowing for filtering at line rate.34

| Enforcement Point | Processing Layer | Context Switching | Performance Potential |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Userspace Proxy | Application Layer | High (Kernel \<-\> User) | Limited; prone to bottlenecks in high-traffic. |
| Kernel Socket | Transport Layer | Moderate | Good for per-application monitoring. |
| eBPF / TC | Network Layer | Low | High; ideal for statistical aggregation. |
| XDP (eBPF) | Data Link Layer | Zero | Ultra-high; processes packets at the NIC driver level. |

The xLSTM architecture is particularly well-suited for this environment. Unlike graph-based models that require recursive parsing of resolution chains, xLSTM learns temporal dependencies directly from packet sequences.38 This enables real-time DNS tunnel detection on commodity hardware with a per-sample latency of 0.041 ms, which is an order of magnitude faster than traditional deep learning approaches.38

### **Multi-Layered Caching and Latency Mitigation**

Inference is probabilistic and computationally variable, meaning that identical requests can produce different response times depending on the model's state and batching strategies.39 To maintain a deterministic and fast user experience, autonomous systems implement multi-layered caching.40

A common architecture involves:

1. **L1 Cache (Client-Side):** Slashed configuration fetch latency by over 98%, reducing typical 400ms bottlenecks to sub-millisecond responses.40  
2. **L2 Cache (Server-Side):** Acts as a resilience buffer. If the AI inference engine is saturated or the reputation database is temporarily unreachable, the L2 cache serves safe, previously validated responses.40  
3. **Peer-to-Peer Storage:** For high-throughput environments, a peer-to-peer storage architecture allows multiple nodes to simultaneously transfer reputation data directly to GPU memory using APIs like GPUDirect, eliminating CPU involvement in data transfers.41

This caching strategy ensures that the system is not just fast, but also resilient. In the event of a database outage, the L2 cache allows AI inference workflows to continue uninterrupted, preventing a total service failure.40

## **Comparative Performance of Machine Learning Classifiers**

The selection of the appropriate machine learning classifier is a critical decision in the design of an autonomous DNS blocker. The research indicates that while deep learning (DL) models offer superior pattern recognition, traditional ML models like Random Forest (RF) often provide the best balance of accuracy and computational efficiency for real-time applications.42

### **Evaluation Metrics in DNS Security**

The efficacy of a DNS classifier is typically measured using Precision (![][image6]), Recall (![][image7]), and the ![][image8] score. These metrics are defined as follows:

![][image9]  
In these formulas, ![][image10] stands for True Positives, ![][image11] for False Positives, and ![][image12] for False Negatives. In the context of DNS filtering, a False Positive (blocking a legitimate site) is often considered more damaging than a False Negative (allowing an ad through), as it directly impacts service availability.1

| Algorithm | Accuracy | Precision | Recall | Latency | Recommended Use Case |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Random Forest | 99.33% | 99.2% | 99.1% | Low | Real-time DDoS and tunnel detection. 1 |
| XGBoost | 99.98% | 99.8% | 99.7% | Moderate | High-volume traffic classification. 42 |
| CNN-LSTM | 97.5% | 96.8% | 97.1% | High | Low-throughput data exfiltration detection. 15 |
| Naive Bayes | 85.0% | 82.0% | 84.0% | Very Low | Basic heuristic filtering at the edge. 45 |
| GNN | 99.8% | 99.5% | 99.4% | Very High | Structural analysis of recursive chains. 14 |

The research highlights that "shallow" but high-entropy features, when processed through non-linear meta-learners like CatBoost or LightGBM, can reach near-state-of-the-art performance without the massive resource demands of GPU-accelerated deep learning.44 This "Green AI" approach is essential for deploying autonomous filtering on constrained hardware such as home routers or IoT gateways.44

## **Dataset Preparation and the "Grandma Test"**

The performance of any AI-driven system is fundamentally limited by the quality of its training data. Autonomous DNS blockers must utilize diverse and representative datasets to avoid bias and ensure broad applicability.1

To obtain benign domain data, researchers typically use logs containing queries from a variety of business and educational networks, selecting separate one-hour windows over several days to capture varying traffic patterns.1 Critical preprocessing steps include:

1. **Filtering Top Domains:** The top 500 most queried effective Second Level Domains (SLDs) are often filtered out, as they account for over 50% of traffic and provide minimal learning value for anomaly detection.1  
2. **Deduplication and Validation:** Ensuring that the dataset does not contain duplicate entries that could bias the model toward specific high-volume trackers.47  
3. **Handling Class Imbalance:** Malicious queries are often "under-represented" in real-world traffic. Systems use synthetic oversampling or specialized batching strategies to ensure the model learns to identify rare threats effectively.13

From a configuration perspective, the "grandma test" is a qualitative benchmark for an autonomous adblocker: the system must provide a high level of protection with "few exceptions," ensuring that a non-technical user can enjoy an ad-free experience without ever needing to manually adjust settings or allowlists.48 This is achieved by utilizing the "law of diminishing returns," focusing on high-quality, high-impact blocks while avoiding overly aggressive rules that marginally increase protection at the cost of significantly increased website breakage.48

## **Future Directions: Zero-Trust DNS and Federated Learning**

The evolution of DNS filtering is moving toward a Zero-Trust model, where no domain is considered "safe" by default. Instead, every resolution request is subjected to real-time behavioral and structural analysis.49

The integration of federated learning offers a path toward crowd-sourced intelligence without compromising individual privacy. In such a system, local DNS resolvers (like a Pi-hole or AdGuard Home instance) could learn from local traffic patterns and share model updates—rather than raw DNS logs—with a central server.50 This allows the global model to evolve and catch new threats across the entire network as they emerge on a single node, while keeping user browsing habits local and encrypted.50

As the advertising industry continues to develop encrypted ad delivery and same-domain serving, the role of AI in DNS filtering will only grow. The ability of machine learning to identify the "detailed fingerprint" of a domain—capturing patterns that are invisible to human maintainers—provides a critical advantage in the ongoing effort to secure the internet's most fundamental directory service.4 Through the combination of autonomous monitoring, pre-deployment verification, and periodic integrity checks, the AI-driven DNS adblocker ensures a resilient, high-performance, and privacy-respecting online environment.
